When the user clicks the confirmation action to reschedule, validate that a session is selected and that it is one of the suggestedSessions; then call rescheduleBooking(bookingId, newClassSessionId).

When rescheduleBooking resolves successfully, show a success message using the message and newStartDateTime/newEndDateTime from the response, refresh booking details via getBookingDetail(bookingId), and update the URL to exit reschedule mode while optionally adding a query parameter that indicates a recent successful reschedule.
When rescheduleBooking fails (success is false), show the returned message and keep the reschedule selector visible for the user to try a different option.

When the user dismisses notification messages (success or error), clear the visible notification state but do not modify persistent URL parameters unless the dismissal is specifically tied to exiting reschedule mode.

If the user navigates between different bookings (e.g., via links that change bookingId in the URL), detect the change, reset internal state (including reschedule state and selected session), and repeat the data loading process for the new bookingId.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL query string. The page reads bookingId from the query parameters on load (e.g., booking_detail.html?bookingId=...). If bookingId is missing or empty, the page enters an error state and no booking-related interfaces are called. Once bookingId is present and the user is authenticated, the page calls getBookingDetail(bookingId) to obtain all booking details and reschedule eligibility flags. A separate optional query parameter (e.g., action) is used to represent reschedule mode. When action indicates rescheduling and getBookingDetail.canReschedule is true, the page automatically triggers getRescheduleOptions(bookingId) and opens the reschedule selector. If canReschedule is false, the action parameter is ignored for rescheduling; the page may still show the reschedulePolicySummary explaining the restriction. Another optional query parameter (e.g., selectedSessionId) may be used to reflect the currently chosen newClassSessionId from suggestedSessions; when this parameter is present, the reschedule selector attempts to preselect the matching session. The page ensures any selectedSessionId corresponds to a session returned in suggestedSessions; otherwise, it is cleared from in-memory state and may be removed from the URL. A one-time query parameter (e.g., rescheduleSuccess) can be appended to the URL after a successful reschedule so that on reload the page can show a success banner; after the banner is rendered and dismissed, the parameter can be cleaned up by a navigation that updates the URL without reloading the entire page if the framework allows. All transient interaction state (such as loading flags, inline validation errors, and open/closed panel state) is maintained in memory within components and is not stored in localStorage or other persistent browser storage.

components.functionality: Checks whether the user is authenticated before allowing booking details and reschedule actions to load and render.
Loads booking details for the bookingId found in the URL and exposes them to child components.
Displays high-level booking information including class title, booking type, date and time range, location summary, and participant count.
Shows booking status, payment information, and time-until-start indicators to help the user understand the current state and reschedule window.
Provides navigation back to the My Bookings page and indicates that the user is viewing a specific booking.
Checks reschedule eligibility based on booking details and offers a reschedule button when allowed; otherwise surfaces the policy explanation.
When reschedule mode is active, loads possible future sessions and policy boundaries for rescheduling the booking.
Displays suggested future sessions for rescheduling and allows the user to choose one session as the new booking time.
Confirms the reschedule action by sending the selected session id to the backend and handling success or failure responses.
Displays transient success or error messages based on outcomes of data loading and reschedule actions, including messages from API responses.
Optionally surfaces additional metadata such as notes or timestamps pulled from booking details, without adding any static content.

name: Compare Workshops
title: Compare Workshops
description: Side-by-side comparison page for workshops the user has added to their comparison list. Uses the workshop compare state to show key details, highlight cheaper options, and let users navigate to class detail pages or back to workshop listings.

core_features: Load the current workshop comparison list using getWorkshopCompareState and, when non-empty, retrieve detailed comparison data using getWorkshopComparisonView.

Display each workshop in a side-by-side comparison table, showing key attributes returned by getWorkshopComparisonView such as title, categoryName, level, locationSummary, durationMinutes, priceDisplay, ratingAverage, ratingCount, typicalTimeOfDay, primaryWeekday, and nextAvailableDate.
Highlight price differences using the isCheapest flag from getWorkshopComparisonView so users can quickly identify cheaper options.
Provide clickable workshop titles in the comparison table that navigate to class_detail.html with the appropriate id URL parameter (classOfferingId).
Allow users to remove individual workshops from the comparison via removeWorkshopFromCompare, updating the table dynamically based on the returned compare state.
Allow users to clear the entire comparison list via clearWorkshopCompareList, then show an appropriate empty-state message and a link back to the workshops listing.
Provide a "Back to all workshops" navigation control that links to classes.html with categoryId=workshops to let users continue browsing.
user_workflows:
Compare selected workshops side-by-side:
1) User arrives on compare.html from the classes listing compare bar.
2) On page load, the page calls getWorkshopCompareState.
3) If the returned count is greater than zero, the page calls getWorkshopComparisonView to get detailed comparison data for all workshops in the list.
4) The page renders a comparison table with one column per workshop using the workshops array from getWorkshopComparisonView.
5) The user scans attributes such as priceDisplay, durationMinutes, level, locationSummary, typicalTimeOfDay, primaryWeekday, nextAvailableDate, and ratings.
6) Columns where isCheapest is true are visually indicated as cheaper options.
7) The user can click a workshop title to navigate to class_detail.html?id={classOfferingId} for more information and booking.

Remove a single workshop from the comparison:
1) While viewing compare.html, the user decides one workshop is no longer relevant.
2) The user clicks a "Remove" control associated with that workshop column.
3) The page calls removeWorkshopFromCompare with the workshop's classOfferingId.
4) The interface returns the updated offeringIds and count.
5) If count is still greater than zero, the page calls getWorkshopComparisonView again to refresh the comparison data.
6) The comparison table re-renders with the remaining workshops; if count becomes zero, the page switches to the empty-state view.

Clear all workshops from comparison:
1) The user clicks a "Clear all" control on the compare page.
2) The page calls clearWorkshopCompareList (no parameters).
3) The interface returns an empty offeringIds array and count=0.
4) The page stops showing the comparison table and shows an empty-state message instead.
5) The user can then click the "Back to all workshops" link, navigating to classes.html?categoryId=workshops to select new workshops to compare.

Return to workshop listings from comparison:
1) At any time, the user can click a "Back to all workshops" control.
2) The page navigates the browser to classes.html with the URL parameter categoryId=workshops.
3) The workshops listings page can then show the workshops category and (outside the scope of this page) allow the user to add workshops back into comparison.
interactions: On page load, automatically call getWorkshopCompareState to determine whether there are workshops to compare and how many.
If getWorkshopCompareState returns count > 0, immediately call getWorkshopComparisonView to fetch the workshops array and populate the comparison table.
If getWorkshopCompareState returns count = 0, skip calling getWorkshopComparisonView and show an empty-state panel with a link to classes.html?categoryId=workshops.
Each workshop column in the comparison table includes a clickable title; clicking it navigates to class_detail.html?id={classOfferingId} using the classOfferingId from the workshops array.

Each workshop column includes a "Remove" action; clicking it triggers removeWorkshopFromCompare(classOfferingId), then the page updates its internal compare state using the returned offeringIds and count, and re-calls getWorkshopComparisonView when count > 0.

A top-level or footer "Clear all" action is available when count > 0; clicking it triggers clearWorkshopCompareList(), then switches the UI to the empty-state view using the returned empty offeringIds and count.
A persistent "Back to all workshops" link is available; clicking it navigates to classes.html?categoryId=workshops without modifying the compare state.

When getWorkshopComparisonView is called, the page uses isCheapest in each workshop item to conditionally display a "Cheapest" indicator or other emphasis, without performing additional price calculations on the client.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page does not accept incoming URL parameters for its own data fetching; instead, it relies on the single-user server-side comparison state accessed via getWorkshopCompareState. On initial load, the client calls getWorkshopCompareState. If the returned count is greater than zero, the client calls getWorkshopComparisonView to obtain the workshops array for rendering. The internal page state consists of: (1) currentCompareIds, derived from the offeringIds returned by getWorkshopCompareState, and (2) currentComparisonData, derived from the workshops array returned by getWorkshopComparisonView. These are kept in sync by re-calling getWorkshopCompareState and getWorkshopComparisonView after any removeWorkshopFromCompare or clearWorkshopCompareList operation. URL parameters are used only for navigation targets, not for persisting the comparison list: clicking a workshop title navigates to class_detail.html?id={classOfferingId}, passing the selected workshop's classOfferingId as the id URL parameter; clicking "Back to all workshops" navigates to classes.html?categoryId=workshops, using the categoryId URL parameter to indicate the workshops listing context. No localStorage or client-side URL encoding of offeringIds is used for compare state; all such state is managed through the provided interfaces.

name: About
title: About
description: Page that loads and displays mission, teaching philosophy, safety and quality information, instructor profiles, and studio locations using the about-page content API, with navigation to browse all classes.

core_features: Fetch and display About page content (headline, mission, teaching philosophy, safety and quality text, instructor list, studio list) using getAboutPageContent.
Show a structured overview of the art education service’s purpose and mission based on API-provided text content.
Present teaching philosophy content to help users understand the approach to art education.
Present safety and quality information to support trust-building.
Display a list of instructors with their bios and specialties, allowing users to expand/collapse detailed bios.
Display a list of studio locations with names and addresses.
Provide optional in-page navigation to jump directly to mission, teaching philosophy, instructors, or studios sections using URL parameters.
Provide a call-to-action button that navigates to the classes listing page with categoryId=all_classes.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View general About information
1. User navigates to about.html (e.g., via footer About link).
2. On page load, the root component calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
3. While data is loading, the page can show a loading state (e.g., generic loading indicators without hardcoded content).
4. Once the API returns, the headline, mission, teaching philosophy, safety/quality text, instructor list, and studio list are bound to their respective components.
5. The user scrolls the page to read mission, teaching philosophy, and safety/quality sections.

Workflow 2: View instructor details
1. User is on about.html with content already loaded from getAboutPageContent.
2. In the instructor list, each instructor entry shows summary information (e.g., name and primary specialties) and a control to expand the full bio.
3. User clicks an instructor’s-expand control (e.g., a button or clickable row).
4. The instructor card toggles a local open/closed state to show or hide the full bio text from getAboutPageContent.instructors[i].bio.
5. Optional: The component updates the URL query parameter (e.g., ?section=instructors&instructorIndex=n) without reloading the page, allowing the user to share a deep link that opens the same instructor expanded.
6. If another instructor is clicked, that card toggles independently; the URL parameter is updated accordingly.

Workflow 3: View studio locations
1. User scrolls to or navigates to the studios section of about.html.
2. The studios list component reads the studios array from getAboutPageContent.studios and renders each studio’s name and address.
3. User may click on a studio list item to focus it or to copy the address (handled by standard browser interactions; no additional API calls are made).

Workflow 4: Use URL parameters to jump to a section
1. User opens a link to about.html that optionally includes a section query parameter, for example: about.html?section=instructors.
2. The page loads and calls getAboutPageContent as usual (no dependence on the URL for data fetching).
3. After data is loaded, the root component reads window.location.search and determines the requested section value (e.g., mission, teaching, safety, instructors, studios).
4. The page scrolls/focuses to the corresponding section component.
5. If the section parameter corresponds to instructors and an additional instructorIndex parameter is present, the instructor list component opens the matching instructor card by default.

Workflow 5: Navigate to browse all classes
1. User reads the About content and is interested in available classes.
2. User clicks the 'Browse all classes' call-to-action button within the About page.
3. The click handler constructs a URL to classes.html including the required query parameter categoryId=all_classes.
4. Browser navigates to classes.html?categoryId=all_classes.
5. The classes page (outside the scope of this specification) uses that parameter to determine which listings to show.
interactions:
Click: Instructor card expand/collapse
- Target: Expand/collapse control on each instructor entry.
- Behavior: Toggles a boolean state for that instructor card (expanded vs collapsed) to show or hide the full bio. Does not trigger any new API calls.
- Optional URL state: On expand, updates the query string (e.g., ?section=instructors&instructorIndex=n) via history.pushState or history.replaceState so the current view can be shared.
- On page load, if instructorIndex is present and valid, the corresponding instructor is expanded by default.

Click: Section navigation links
- Target: In-page navigation items (e.g., links to mission, teaching, safety, instructors, studios sections).
- Behavior: When a section link is clicked, the handler updates the URL query parameter section to the corresponding value and scrolls to that section without reloading the page.
- Example: Clicking the 'Instructors' link updates the URL to about.html?section=instructors and scrolls to the instructors section component.

Click: 'Browse all classes' CTA
- Target: A button or link within the About page.
- Behavior: On click, navigates to classes.html with the query parameter categoryId set to all_classes.
- Implementation: window.location.href = 'classes.html?categoryId=all_classes'.

Lifecycle: Page load and data fetch
- On initial page load (and on any subsequent soft navigation to about.html), the root page component calls getAboutPageContent with no arguments.
- While waiting for a response, the page shows generic loading states (e.g., loading indicators) instead of any hardcoded About content.
- Once data is received, the state is updated with the API response, which populates all bound components (headline, mission, teachingPhilosophy, safetyAndQuality, instructors, studios).

Behavior: URL parameter change handling
- When the section or instructorIndex query parameters change via in-page navigation, the page does not re-call getAboutPageContent; it only updates local UI state (which section is focused and which instructor is expanded).
- If the user manually edits the URL parameters and presses Enter, the page re-parses the parameters on load and adjusts scroll/expansion state accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page always fetches its content from getAboutPageContent without relying on any URL parameters for data fetching. The URL is used only for client-side state (navigation and deep-linking). The page recognizes optional query parameters: `section` and `instructorIndex`. The `section` parameter can have values like `mission`, `teaching`, `safety`, `instructors`, or `studios`. After getAboutPageContent resolves, the page checks `section` and scrolls/focuses the corresponding section component. If `section=instructors` and `instructorIndex` is a valid zero-based index within the instructors array, the instructor-list component initializes with that instructor card in an expanded state. Changes to in-page navigation update these parameters via history APIs without reloading the page. Outgoing navigation to classes.html uses a fixed query parameter `categoryId=all_classes` to indicate that all classes should be shown; this parameter is not read on the About page itself.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page, fetches data from getAboutPageContent on load, manages global loading and error states, parses URL parameters for section-level navigation, and passes data and state to child components.
Displays the main About page headline text provided by the API. This is functional context, not a marketing hero banner.
Shows the mission text describing the purpose and goals of the art education service. Supports deep-linking via the `section=mission` URL parameter.
Displays the teaching philosophy text from the API, explaining how classes are taught and the approach to art education. Supports deep-linking via `section=teaching`.
Presents safety and quality-related information from the API, including any policies or standards described in the safetyAndQuality field. Supports deep-linking via `section=safety`.

Renders a list of instructor profiles based on getAboutPageContent.instructors. Each list item shows instructor name and specialties with the ability to expand/collapse the full bio. Supports initialization and deep-linking via `section=instructors` and `instructorIndex` URL parameters.
Represents a single instructor in the list. Handles local expanded/collapsed state to show or hide the bio and exposes an event to update URL parameters when expanded.
Displays a list of studio locations from getAboutPageContent.studios, showing each studio’s name and address. Supports deep-linking via `section=studios`.
Represents a single studio entry. Displays API-provided studio name and address. May support basic interactions such as focusing or copying address using default browser behavior.

Provides in-page navigation links to jump between mission, teaching philosophy, safety, instructors, and studios sections. Updates the `section` (and optionally `instructorIndex`) query parameters without reloading the page and initiates scrolling to the appropriate component.
Provides a call-to-action control that navigates the user from the About page to the classes listing view, ensuring the URL parameter categoryId=all_classes is set.
Shows a generic loading indicator while getAboutPageContent is in progress. Hides itself once data is successfully loaded or an error occurs.
Displays a functional error message and optional retry control if getAboutPageContent fails. Does not rely on static About content; only generic error copy is used.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where users can view contact details for the art education service and submit inquiries or special requests through a contact form.

core_features: Fetch and display contact details (email, phone, studio address, support hours, response time information) using getContactPageContent.
Provide a contact form that lets users choose an inquiry topic and submit their name, email, phone, and message via submitContactRequest.

Support specific request types (such as class questions, booking questions, group bookings, or special events) through a configurable topic field and message guidance, all funneled into submitContactRequest.topic and submitContactRequest.message.
Show real-time form validation feedback for required fields before submitContactRequest is called.
Show submission status (in progress, success, failure) based on submitContactRequest response without leaving the page.
Use URL parameters to preselect a topic and optionally pre-fill contact fields, and to reflect submission status for deep links or redirects.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – View contact details only:
1) User navigates to contact.html from the footer or any other link.
2) On page load, the page controller calls getContactPageContent with no parameters.
3) When getContactPageContent resolves, the contact-info-panel displays the returned email, phone, studioAddress, supportHours, and responseTimeInfo.
4) User may use the displayed information directly (e.g., click the email link to open their mail client, tap the phone number on mobile, or copy the studio address). No form submission is required in this workflow.

Workflow 2 – Submit a general question or class-related inquiry:
1) User opens contact.html, possibly with no URL parameters.
2) The page controller calls getContactPageContent to populate contact details and response expectations.
3) The contact-form initializes with default internal state for topic, name, email, phone, and message. If no topic is provided via URL, the form uses its default topic configuration.
4) User selects an appropriate topic, enters their name and email, optionally phone, and types their question in the message field.
5) On form submit, client-side validation checks that required fields (topic, name, email, message) are present and that email is syntactically valid. If validation fails, error states are shown and submitContactRequest is not called.
6) If validation passes, the contact-form sets its state to “submitting” and calls submitContactRequest with the current form values (topic, name, email, phone, message).
7) While waiting for the response, the submit button is disabled and a submitting indicator is shown.
8) When submitContactRequest returns, the contact-status-banner reads the response object. If success is true, the banner shows a success state and the form may optionally clear or keep values depending on configuration. If success is false, an error state is shown with the returned message.
9) Optionally, the page controller can update the URL (e.g., append a status parameter) so that a refresh or shareable link preserves the success/error state.

Workflow 3 – Submit a group booking or special event request with preselected topic via URL:
1) User clicks a “Contact us about this” link from another page that navigates to contact.html with a topic URL parameter (for example, contact.html?topic=group_booking) and possibly other context parameters (such as a ref or context string). The exact parameter values are not hardcoded in this spec, only the param names and their effects.
2) On page load, the contact-page-controller parses URL parameters. If a topic parameter is present, it sets the initial topic state in the contact-form to match that value (if it is among the configured topic options). If other context parameters (e.g., ref, context) are present, the controller can pre-append or suggest that content into the message field.
3) The controller calls getContactPageContent to populate contact info and response metadata.
4) The contact-form displays with the topic already selected based on the topic URL parameter. The user fills in name, email, phone, and adds event-specific details in the message (e.g., date preferences, group size), possibly guided by helper text in the UI. Any extra structured fields configured for this type of inquiry are merged into the final message text client-side before calling submitContactRequest.
5) On submit, validation runs as in Workflow 2. If validation passes, submitContactRequest is called with the derived topic, name, email, phone, and aggregated message content.
6) After submitContactRequest responds, the contact-status-banner shows a success or error state. The page controller may also sync a status parameter into the URL (e.g., status=success or status=error and an optional code) so that if the user reloads the page, the banner can be restored using the URL state.
interactions: On page load, the contact-page-controller automatically invokes getContactPageContent and sets a loading state until the data resolves or fails.

If getContactPageContent succeeds, the contact-info-panel binds the returned email, phone, and studioAddress fields to clickable UI elements (e.g., dynamic mailto and tel links and an address block). It does not render fallback values when data is missing; instead, it conditionally hides elements with no data.
The contact-response-metadata component binds to supportHours and responseTimeInfo from getContactPageContent and displays them so users understand when to expect a reply.

The topic selector inside the contact-form updates an internal topic state when the user changes the selection. This state is used both for validation and as the topic argument for submitContactRequest.
Name, email, phone, and message fields all update the form’s internal state on input change. The email field includes basic client-side format validation that runs on blur and before submit.

On clicking the form’s submit button, the contact-form triggers its onSubmit handler. This handler performs validation; if invalid, it sets field-level error flags and blocks the call to submitContactRequest.

If validation passes, the onSubmit handler sets the submission state to “submitting”, constructs the message payload (including any derived content from optional event-specific fields or URL context), and calls submitContactRequest(topic, name, email, phone, message).
While submitContactRequest is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a submitting indicator is displayed. Additional submissions are blocked until the current call resolves.

When submitContactRequest resolves, the contact-status-banner reads the returned success and message. If success is true, it shows a success status and may clear non-persistent validation errors. If success is false, it shows an error status and may provide the returned message as additional detail.

The contact-status-banner includes a dismiss action. When the user dismisses it, the banner hides and, if the URL contains any status-related parameters, the contact-url-state-sync component can remove or update those parameters.

On initial load or whenever the URL changes, the contact-url-state-sync component reads URL parameters such as topic, name, email, phone, context, and status. It uses these values to pre-populate the form’s fields and, if configured, to show an initial status banner without requiring a new submission.

If the user manually modifies the topic or other pre-filled fields, the new values only affect client-side state and do not change the URL unless the implementation chooses to sync them back through the contact-url-state-sync component.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact page derives its behavior both from API responses and from URL parameters. It does not rely on localStorage or other persistent client storage. On initialization, the contact-page-controller reads URL query parameters and initializes internal state accordingly. A `topic` parameter (if present) is used to select the initial topic in the contact-form, provided its value matches a configured topic option. Parameters such as `name`, `email`, `phone`, or a generic `context` parameter can be used to pre-fill the corresponding form fields or append contextual text to the message field; these values are treated as initial state only and can be edited by the user. Additional URL parameters such as `status` and optional error or reference parameters can be used to control the contact-status-banner: for example, if `status` indicates a previous successful submission, the banner may initialize in a success state even before any new submitContactRequest call is made. After a submission completes, the page may update the URL to reflect the new status (for example, to enable shareable confirmation URLs) by adding or updating these parameters. In all cases, the live contact details (email, phone, studioAddress, supportHours, responseTimeInfo) are sourced exclusively from the getContactPageContent interface, and the submitContactRequest interface is the sole mechanism for sending user inquiries from this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and URL-based state, calling getContactPageContent on page load, parsing URL parameters, and distributing initial state (contact info, pre-selected topic, pre-filled fields, and any status indicators) to child components.

Displays dynamic contact details for the art education service, including email, phone, and studio address. Provides actionable links based on these values, such as an email link and a phone link, using only the data returned by getContactPageContent.

Shows support hours and typical response time information so users know when they can expect a reply to their inquiry. This component is read-only and purely reflects the data from getContactPageContent.

Collects the user’s inquiry details (topic, name, email, phone, message), performs client-side validation, and submits the data via submitContactRequest. It supports initialization from URL parameters and from the controller’s state, and handles its own field-level error states and submission state.

Shows the outcome of a contact form submission or a pre-existing status indicated by URL parameters. It displays success or error states based on submitContactRequest responses and can be dismissed by the user.

Keeps important parts of the Contact page state synchronized with the URL query parameters without using localStorage. On load, it reads parameters to initialize the form and status banner; after submissions, it can update or clear status-related parameters to reflect the latest result.

name: FAQ
title: FAQ
description: Provides categorized, searchable answers to common questions about classes, bookings, payments, passes, gift cards, and promo codes.

core_features: Load all FAQ entries from the getFaqContent interface on page initialization.
Group FAQ entries by their categoryCode to allow users to focus on specific topics (for example, booking-related or payment-related questions).
Filter visible FAQ entries by category using a URL-driven category selection (e.g., faq.html?category=...).
Provide a text search input that filters the loaded FAQ entries client-side by question and answer content, synchronized with a URL query parameter (e.g., faq.html?q=...).
Display FAQ questions in an expandable/collapsible list (accordion-style) where expanding a question reveals its full answer.
Maintain which question is expanded via URL parameters so that specific questions can be deep-linked and restored on page reload (e.g., faq.html?expandedId=...).
Handle empty or no-match states when filters/search return zero FAQ entries, with guidance to adjust filters or search terms.
Allow users to copy or share a direct link to a specific question using the current URL including relevant query parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Browse FAQs by category
1) User navigates to faq.html (optionally with a category URL parameter).
2) The page calls getFaqContent and receives an array of FAQ entries.
3) The FAQ entries are grouped by categoryCode.
4) If a category URL parameter is present and matches one of the categoryCode values, that category is considered active; otherwise an all-categories view or default category is used.
5) The category filter component highlights the active category and filters the list of FAQ entries accordingly.
6) User clicks a different category; the page updates the category URL parameter and re-renders the FAQ list for that category without re-calling getFaqContent (client-side filtering).

Workflow: Search within FAQs
1) User opens faq.html; getFaqContent is called and FAQ entries are stored in page state.
2) User types a search phrase into the search input and submits the form.
3) The page updates the q URL parameter to the search phrase.
4) The FAQ list component filters the already-loaded FAQ entries using the current q term (and any active category) against question and answer text.
5) Matching entries are displayed; non-matching entries are hidden.
6) If the user clears the search input and submits, the q URL parameter is removed or set to empty, and the full filtered-by-category list is shown again.

Workflow: Open and deep-link to a specific question
1) User arrives at faq.html with a URL that may include expandedId and category parameters.
2) The page calls getFaqContent, groups entries by categoryCode, and applies category and q filters as indicated by URL parameters.
3) The FAQ list component checks expandedId in the URL and, if it matches one of the FAQ entries (e.g., via a derived stable ID), marks that question as expanded and scrolls it into view.
4) User clicks on a different question to expand it; the component collapses the previously expanded question (if any).
5) The page updates the expandedId URL parameter to match the newly expanded question so that the current open question is encoded in the URL.
6) User copies the browser URL and shares it; when another user opens that URL, steps 1–3 restore the same expanded question.

Workflow: Recover from no results
1) User selects a category and/or enters a search term that produces zero matches in the FAQ list.
2) The FAQ list component detects that the filtered result set is empty.
3) An empty-state message is shown with suggestions such as adjusting filters or search terms.
4) User modifies the search text or category; the q or category URL parameter is updated correspondingly.
5) As soon as the new filters produce matching entries, the FAQ list is rendered normally and the empty state is hidden.
interactions: Category selection: Clicking a category control updates the active category in state and synchronizes it with a category URL parameter; the FAQ list re-filters client-side.
Search input: Typing text and submitting the search form updates the q URL parameter and triggers client-side filtering of FAQ entries by question and answer.
Clear search: Interacting with a clear/reset control removes or empties the q URL parameter and restores the unfiltered (by search) FAQ list.

Expand/collapse question: Clicking on a question header toggles its expanded state, reveals or hides the answer, and updates the expandedId URL parameter to reflect the current open question (or removes it if none is open).

Deep-link handling: On page load or when the URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation), the state-sync logic reads URL parameters and updates active category, search term, and expanded question without re-calling the backend unless necessary.
Retry on load failure: If getFaqContent fails, a retry control calls getFaqContent again when clicked.
page_functionality.state_logic: State is managed using URL query parameters combined with in-memory page state. The primary URL parameters are: (1) category: stores the current category filter and is set when the user selects a category; if missing or invalid, the page either shows all categories or falls back to a default category derived from the loaded data. (2) q: stores the current search term entered into the search input; when present, the FAQ list filters entries by matching this term against question and answer text, in addition to any category filter; when q is removed or empty, search filtering is disabled. (3) expandedId: stores the identifier of the FAQ question that is currently expanded; this ID is derived deterministically from the question (for example, based on its index in the loaded array or a hash) and is used on load to re-expand the correct question; if expandedId does not match any current entry in the filtered list, no question is expanded. All filtering and expand/collapse behavior operates on the single dataset loaded from getFaqContent; the interface is called on initial page load and on explicit retry actions, but not for each filter or interaction. Browser back/forward navigation is respected by listening for URL changes and re-applying filters and expansion state from the current URL parameters.

components.functionality: Fetches FAQ entries from getFaqContent on initial page load (and on retry), stores the resulting array in page-level state, and exposes loading, success, and error states to other components.

Reads and writes FAQ-related URL parameters (category, q, expandedId). On initialization and on browser navigation events, it parses the current URL parameters and updates page state for active category, search term, and expanded question. When internal state changes (category selection, search submission, question expansion), it updates the URL parameters without reloading the page.

Displays available FAQ categories derived from the categoryCode values present in the loaded FAQ entries and allows the user to select one. When a category is selected, it updates the active category in page state and triggers a URL update for the category parameter.

Provides a text input for searching within FAQ questions and answers. It initializes the input value from the q URL parameter, updates internal state as the user types, and on submit updates the q URL parameter. It triggers re-filtering of the FAQ list client-side based on the current search term.

Renders the list of FAQ entries as an expandable/collapsible list. It receives the full FAQ dataset from faq-data-loader and the current filters (category, q) from page state or faq-url-state-sync. It filters the dataset client-side, orders the results if needed, and for each entry shows the question as a clickable header and the answer in an expandable panel. It initializes which panel is open based on expandedId from the URL and updates expandedId when a different question is toggled.

Observes the filtered FAQ list (after applying category and search filters). When the filtered result set is empty and data loading has succeeded, it hides the FAQ list and displays guidance to adjust category or search, without performing additional API calls.
Displays an error message and retry control if getFaqContent fails. It listens to the error state from faq-data-loader and, when the user clicks retry, triggers a new call to getFaqContent.

For each visible FAQ question, provides a control (for example, a link icon) that, when activated, ensures the current question is marked as expanded, updates the expandedId URL parameter accordingly, and exposes the resulting URL so the user can copy or share it. It relies on the same ID scheme and URL state used by faq-url-state-sync.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Displays the current legal Terms & Conditions for using the site and services, using dynamic content from the terms API.

core_features: Fetch and display the latest Terms & Conditions content using the getTermsContent interface.
Show the last-updated timestamp associated with the Terms & Conditions.
Allow deep-linking to specific parts of the Terms & Conditions content via URL parameters (e.g., to scroll to a specific section anchor).
Support in-page navigation to anchors contained within the Terms & Conditions content (e.g., booking, cancellations, refunds) without hardcoding section names.
Provide utility actions such as printing the Terms & Conditions and copying a direct link to a specific section.
user_workflows: View current Terms & Conditions: 1) User clicks the “Terms & Conditions” link in the footer. 2) Browser navigates to terms.html (optionally with URL parameters). 3) On page load, the page invokes getTermsContent with no parameters. 4) On success, the page displays the last-updated timestamp and renders the returned body text as sanitized rich content. 5) User scrolls and reads the content.

Open Terms & Conditions at a specific section via link: 1) User receives or clicks a link to terms.html with a section parameter (e.g., terms.html?section=some-section-id). 2) On page load, the page invokes getTermsContent. 3) Once the content is rendered, the page looks for an element within the terms body whose id matches the section parameter. 4) If found, the page scrolls that element into view and optionally applies a temporary highlight. 5) User reads the section and can scroll to the rest of the terms.

Highlight a term or phrase from URL: 1) User follows a link to terms.html with a highlight parameter (e.g., terms.html?highlight=refund). 2) On page load, the page invokes getTermsContent. 3) After rendering, a client-side function searches within the rendered body text for the highlight phrase. 4) All occurrences are wrapped with a highlight style (implemented purely client-side, without altering the source data). 5) The page scrolls to the first match if available.

Print Terms & Conditions: 1) User scrolls or moves focus to the utility actions area. 2) User clicks the 'Print' action. 3) The page triggers the browser’s print dialog, printing the currently rendered Terms & Conditions, including last-updated information.

Copy a link to a specific section: 1) User clicks on or focuses an in-content anchor (e.g., a heading that represents a section). 2) A section-link control associated with that anchor becomes available. 3) User activates the control. 4) The page composes a URL to terms.html with the section parameter set to the anchor’s id and writes it to the clipboard. 5) User can share this URL; when visited, the deep-link workflow runs and scrolls to that section.
interactions: On page load: Trigger a call to getTermsContent, then render lastUpdated and body once data is returned.
On fetch error: Display a non-hardcoded, generic error state (e.g., via a message container) and provide a retry control that re-invokes getTermsContent.
On scroll: Track whether the user has reached the end of the content; this can be used to update UI state (e.g., a 'You’ve reached the end' marker) without affecting other systems.

On anchor click within the terms body: Intercept clicks on in-content anchors that reference fragment IDs within the same page, prevent full page reloads when appropriate, and perform smooth scrolling to the target element.
On 'Print' click: Call the browser’s print mechanism to print the currently displayed terms.
On 'Copy section link' click: Read the target section’s id, update or create the section URL parameter, construct a full URL, and copy it to the clipboard.

On URL parameter change (via navigation or manual edit): Re-run the deep-link and highlight logic after content is loaded, without additional calls to getTermsContent unless the page is fully reloaded.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Terms & Conditions page uses URL query parameters to control client-side state and behavior without storing state in localStorage or similar. The primary parameters are: 1) section: When present (e.g., terms.html?section=some-id), after getTermsContent returns and the body is rendered, the page searches the rendered content for an element whose id equals the section value. If found, the page scrolls this element into view and can transiently highlight it. If not found, no special scroll action occurs and the user remains at the top of the page. This parameter does not change which content version is loaded; it only affects scrolling within the currently loaded content. 2) highlight: When present (e.g., terms.html?highlight=some+phrase), after getTermsContent returns and the body is rendered, the page executes a client-side search within the rendered text for the provided phrase and visually highlights matches (e.g., with a background style) without altering the original content source. The first match, if any, can be scrolled into view. This is a purely client-side enhancement. 3) Additional parameters: The page does not introduce any other required URL parameters; any unknown parameters are ignored by the page logic. The getTermsContent interface does not take parameters, so these URL parameters never affect which data is fetched, only how it is presented. State transitions: a) Initial load: On first visit, the page parses the current URL, stores the parsed section and highlight values in in-memory state, then calls getTermsContent. b) After data load: Once data is loaded and rendered, the page applies deep-link and highlight behavior based on the parsed parameters. c) On in-page navigation that changes the target section (e.g., when the user clicks a 'copy section link' control), the page updates the URL’s section parameter via the History API (pushState or replaceState) and re-runs the scroll/highlight logic as needed, without a new call to getTermsContent. The underlying Terms & Conditions data is always sourced fresh from getTermsContent per page load, and no static defaults are embedded in the page.

components.functionality: Initializes the Terms & Conditions page by parsing URL parameters, calling getTermsContent, and coordinating the initial render and post-render behaviors (deep-link scrolling and text highlighting).
Displays metadata about the Terms & Conditions, specifically the lastUpdated value returned from getTermsContent, so users can see when the terms were most recently updated.

Renders the Terms & Conditions body returned by getTermsContent as sanitized rich content (e.g., HTML or formatted text), supports internal anchor navigation, and exposes hooks for scroll tracking and highlight application.

Uses the section URL parameter to locate the corresponding element within the rendered Terms & Conditions content (by matching id) and scroll it into view, optionally applying a temporary visual emphasis. Reacts both on initial load and when the section parameter changes via in-page history updates.

Reads the highlight URL parameter and, after the terms body is rendered, searches for the specified phrase within the content. It applies a non-destructive visual highlight to all matches and scrolls to the first match if one exists. It does not modify the source data from getTermsContent.

Provides utility controls related to the Terms & Conditions content, including a print action that triggers the browser’s print dialog and a section-link action used in coordination with anchors inside the content to copy a deep-link URL for the current section.
Shows a non-hardcoded error message and a retry control when getTermsContent fails. When the user chooses to retry, the component reinvokes getTermsContent through the page-init logic.

Tracks how far the user has scrolled through the Terms & Conditions content (e.g., as a simple percentage or as a 'reached-end' flag). This state can be used for subtle UX cues, such as indicating that the end of the document has been reached.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the current privacy policy, including data practices and user rights, using content loaded from the privacy policy API.

core_features: Fetch and display the latest privacy policy content using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.
Show the policy's last updated date returned by the API so users know which version they are viewing.
Render the full policy body text (including any headings, lists, or links) returned by the API.
Allow deep-linking to specific policy sections using URL parameters (e.g., ?section=some-section-id) and scroll to the requested section after content loads.
Provide an explicit interaction that helps users start a privacy-related inquiry (for example, linking to a contact page or opening an email client), as directed by the policy content.
Handle loading and error states for the privacy policy fetch, with options to retry if loading fails.
user_workflows:
View privacy policy from footer:
1. User clicks the 'Privacy Policy' link in the footer.
2. Browser navigates to privacy.html.
3. On page load, the privacy-policy-loader component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
4. The page shows a loading state until the API returns data.
5. On success, the last updated date and policy body are rendered.
6. User reads the policy and may scroll through or use intra-page navigation if available.

Open a specific policy section via URL:
1. User follows a link such as privacy.html?section={SECTION_ID} from another part of the site.
2. Browser loads privacy.html and parses the section URL parameter.
3. The privacy-policy-loader component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
4. After the policy body is rendered, the page attempts to locate an element with id matching the section parameter (e.g., a heading generated from the policy body).
5. If found, the page scrolls/focuses to that section; if not found, the page leaves the scroll position at the top.

Start a privacy-related inquiry:
1. User reads the policy and wants to ask a question or exercise a privacy right.
2. User activates the privacy-contact-link component (e.g., clicks a 'Contact us about privacy' link or button).
3. The component navigates to a dedicated contact page with a URL parameter indicating privacy as the topic (e.g., contact.html?topic=privacy) or triggers the contact method specified in the policy content (such as opening a mailto link contained in the body).
4. User completes the inquiry on the destination page or in their email client.
interactions: On initial page load, the privacy-policy-loader component automatically calls getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters.
If getPrivacyPolicyContent is still pending, a loading indicator is shown and user interactions with policy content are disabled until the request completes.
If getPrivacyPolicyContent fails, an error message is displayed with a user-triggered retry action that re-calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
When the policy body is successfully loaded, the privacy-policy-body component renders the body string as rich text and initializes any in-page anchors or headings.
If a section URL parameter is present, the privacy-policy-body or privacy-section-navigator component scrolls to the matching section after render.
When the user clicks a link generated inside the policy body (for example, a mailto address or a link to an external regulator), normal browser navigation for that link occurs.

When the user activates the privacy-contact-link component, the browser navigates to a contact or support page with a URL parameter indicating that the topic is privacy-related, or uses a contact mechanism embedded in the policy content.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Privacy Policy page does not require URL parameters to function, but supports optional URL parameters to manage in-page state. Primary parameter: `section`. If `section` is present (e.g., privacy.html?section={SECTION_ID}), the page stores this value in a transient in-memory state variable during the current session. After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves and the policy body is rendered, the page attempts to locate a DOM element with an id equal to the `section` value (for example, an auto-generated id based on headings parsed from the policy body). If found, the page scrolls or focuses to this element; if not found, the page remains at the default top-of-page state. Changes to the URL `section` parameter (e.g., via internal navigation that updates the query string) trigger the same lookup and scroll behavior without re-calling getPrivacyPolicyContent. No other persistent state is stored via localStorage or similar; all state is derived from the current URL and the API response from getPrivacyPolicyContent.

components.functionality: On page load, fetches privacy policy data using getPrivacyPolicyContent, manages loading and error states, and passes the resulting data to child components for rendering.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Calls getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters and sets internal state { loading: true, error: null }.
onFetchSuccess: Receives { lastUpdated, body } from getPrivacyPolicyContent, updates internal state { loading: false, error: null, policy: { lastUpdated, body } }, and renders child components.
onFetchError: Receives error from getPrivacyPolicyContent, updates internal state { loading: false, error }, and displays an error view with a retry option.
onRetryClick: Re-invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent and resets state to loading.
onUrlChange: Listens for URL changes affecting the `section` query parameter and informs the privacy-section-navigator to update the current target section.

components.functionality: Displays metadata about the privacy policy, primarily the last updated date returned by the API, so users know which version is current.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataChange: When the parent passes updated policy data, formats and displays the lastUpdated value according to the locale or site settings.

components.functionality: Renders the full privacy policy body string returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent, preserving headings, paragraphs, lists, and links. It also exposes a way for other components to query available heading anchors for in-page navigation.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataChange: When a new body string is provided, parses it (e.g., as HTML or markdown), renders it into the DOM, and assigns stable ids to headings if not already provided by the content.
onAfterRender: Emits a list of detected section anchors (e.g., derived from heading elements) to the privacy-section-navigator so it can build a table of contents or handle section-based scrolling.
onInternalAnchorClick: When a user clicks an internal anchor link generated inside the body, optionally updates the `section` URL parameter to match the anchor target without reloading the page.

components.functionality: Manages navigation to specific sections of the privacy policy, based on heading anchors detected in the policy body and the optional `section` URL parameter.
event_handlers: onAnchorsInitialized: Receives the list of anchors (e.g., { id, label }) from privacy-policy-body and stores them for navigation use.
onSectionParamDetected: When the page controller detects a `section` value in the URL, attempts to scroll to the element with the matching id if it exists; otherwise, it does nothing.

onAnchorClick: When a user selects a section from any generated list of anchors (e.g., a table of contents), scrolls to that section and updates the `section` query parameter in the URL to the anchor id.
onUrlSectionChange: When the `section` parameter changes via browser navigation (back/forward), scrolls to the corresponding section if available.

components.functionality: Provides a clear entry point for users who want to contact the organization about privacy matters, using either a contact URL pattern or a contact mechanism embedded in the policy body.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataChange: Optionally scans the body content for explicit contact instructions (such as a specific email address or URL) and uses them as the primary contact mechanism if found.

onClick: Navigates the user to a contact page with a query parameter indicating a privacy-related topic (e.g., contact.html?topic=privacy) or triggers a contact method directly referenced in the policy content, such as opening a mailto link.
onKeyboardActivate: Supports keyboard activation (e.g., Enter/Space) for accessibility, performing the same behavior as onClick.

components.functionality: Shows appropriate feedback while the policy is loading or if an error occurs when calling getPrivacyPolicyContent, and exposes controls for retrying.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateTrue: When loaderState.loading is true, hides policy content components and displays a loading indicator.
onLoadingStateFalse: When loaderState.loading is false and loaderState.error is null, hides the loading indicator and displays the metadata and body components.
onErrorState: When loaderState.error is not null, displays an error message and a retry button.
onRetryClick: Emits an event to the privacy-policy-loader to re-call getPrivacyPolicyContent.
